title: Fitting under-cabinet LED strip lighting
subtitle: Install low-voltage strip lights beneath kitchen wall units
excerpt: How to fit plug-in or hardwired LED strip lighting under kitchen wall cabinets: choosing the correct strip and driver, cutting the strip to length, fixing with self-adhesive backing, routing the cable neatly, and connecting to a switched plug or fused spur.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. IET Wiring Regulations 18th Edition. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A transformer that converts mains voltage (230 V AC) to the low DC voltage required by LED strip lighting, typically 12 V or 24 V. Must be rated at a wattage at least 20% higher than the total strip wattage to avoid overheating.

A two-digit code describing the protection of an electrical component against dust and moisture. IP20 is for dry indoor use. IP44 and above is required within 600 mm of a sink or other water source in a kitchen.

content.text: Under-cabinet LED strips run on 12 V or 24 V DC supplied by an

plugged into a standard socket or wired to a fused spur. The low-voltage side of the installation is straightforward; the only mains connection is the driver's plug or wired supply, making this one of the more accessible electrical jobs. Check the
of the strip before buying: use IP44 or above near the sink.

LED strip light rated for the run length needed. An LED driver rated at 120% of the total strip wattage minimum. Low-voltage connecting cable and push-in connectors if linking strips around corners. Cable clips or self-adhesive cable management channel for routing. Scissors or a utility knife to cut the strip at the marked cut points.
Step 1, measure and cut the strip

Measure each cabinet section and mark the required length on the strip. LED strips can only be cut at the marked cut lines printed at regular intervals (usually every 50 mm). Cut straight across the strip at a marked line using scissors; cutting between marks severs the circuit and that section will not light. Plan the cuts so each run ends on a cut mark.
Step 2, fix the strips and route the cable

Clean the underside of each cabinet with a dry cloth. Peel the backing from the strip adhesive and press the strip firmly to the cabinet base, keeping it parallel to the front edge at a consistent distance. Route the low-voltage cable up into the cabinet interior through a small hole drilled in the cabinet base, then along to the driver position. Use cable clips to keep the cable out of sight.
Step 3, connect the driver and test

Connect the low-voltage strip cable to the driver output terminals, observing correct polarity (positive to positive, negative to negative). Plug the driver into a switched socket outlet or connect it to a fused spur via the driver's mains cable. Switch on and confirm all sections light. If one section fails, check that the cut was made on a marked line and that the connector is fully seated.
